title: What Steps Can I Take To Master Facial Anatomy？

segments.text: how can i progress my anatomy knowledge you know how can i take the first step on the road to mastery well most people will start with textbooks and I do recommend obviously that's a great place to start you're going to you you're going to pick up different things from different textbooks they're all two-dimensional and that's probably the first thing is most people think that knowing the two-dimensional be able to label all the arteries is it is a good step but you rapidly feel like when you're as soon as you're confronted with the real face you realize this is different it's three-dimensional it's an individual person who the slightly variations in anatomy and that sense of clarity that you get when you can finally label all the arteries which is a good place to start and this appears when you actually confronted with a patient because I think inherently most people realized it's a bit more complex than this like this isn't you've learned the academic and after me but you haven't learned clinical anatomy like it you have to take it to the next level and make it real for this particular patient and that involves um basically an extra step

which is what I recommend you do after you've done your textbook reading is great and but you need to do more but people often ask which are the good textbooks and I think it's fair to say this industry isn't sort of swimming in amazing accessible textbooks it is that right would you say certainly got better right there's certainly more than there were when I started out we could we link below maybe afterwards some links that's probably the one I find it's all it's a cadaver base as well so that called facial topography facial topography I think it's just it I'll link below and but that's one of the most beautiful text books up north those are great pictures team of how much it was I've introduced expensive hundred maybe even three hundred quid or something it's most expensive book back of the book yeah see that's the thing isn't it it's like that we probably gonna go through now like a ladder of the different and increasingly better ways to skill yourself art but if I'm making a decision if you're saying well the first

rung in the ladder it's a textbook and they're not the chisel you know then that's gonna make me think well should I even bother or you know I don't know when they have any thoughts on that I definitely think you need right you should bother and it doesn't have to be the 300 quid version but you need to have some reference that you're starting to integrate this knowledge into your practice maybe but maybe the reason I'm saying that it's because I know that this is not I don't mean this peer plug but let's give me the training we have amazing manuals for people who come and train with us so maybe if would is it enough to have for example our manuals I suppose a lot of people here watching this won't have come to us well it's certainly helpful to having a manual but the next day stage on the processor we're going to say it's actually it's the it's how you utilize that information that day to day practice and that comes from your first training course you should be on those days realizing how anatomical knowledge affects your decision-making right and that that's the most vital component of

it and what happens is you get better as your resolution for those decisions goes up so you mean so you're so it's the resolution of your Anatomy for example like if you look at a diagram of a face you can tell for example that your superior labral artery runs above your lip that's a bit different to knowing to getting the sense that a millimeter or two is going to make the difference between being in it and near it and you starting you start to get that sense like I'm pretty confident at one position them in the Vermilion border that there's no chance I'm gonna be in an artery because I dealt with real human beings you mean yeah it comes from all the factors I mean it helps massively seeing it in a real you know in it that's you know the ultimate the gold standard is cadaver studying a cadaver which were lucky enough to be able to do and to offer but it's actually formed by multiple things like some of it can be a sound bite that goes in on a particular training session you know I remember I still use this analogy myself the Loch Ness monster

so the artery moves like the Loch Ness monster bouncing along the Vermilion border that goes into my head as a snippet that I use to navigate and so it's that kind of thing you might get a snippet from one person talking about anatomy you might get an insight from a photograph in a book and you might gonna could a class and actually see it and all of these things they start to build your mental model yeah so um it's a little bit like if I go back to the analogy of knowing the way if you if someone showed you a map of Manchester and how to get in a black and white map you'd be you'd it would help but a color map might be slightly better you know aerial photograph is from the next step up and then actually walking there mmm would give you a totally different experience [Music]
